1.anchor: Solomon took thirteen years to build his palace.
1.note: tn Heb “His house Solomon built in thirteen years and he completed all his house.”

2.anchor: He named
2.note: tn Heb “he built.”

2.anchor: it “The Palace of the Lebanon Forest”;
2.note: sn The Palace of the Lebanon Forest. This name was appropriate because of the large amount of cedar, undoubtedly brought from Lebanon, used in its construction. The cedar pillars in the palace must have given it the appearance of a forest.

2.anchor: it was 150 feet
2.note: tn Heb “one hundred cubits.”

2.anchor: long, 75 feet
2.note: tn Heb “fifty cubits.”

2.anchor: wide, and 45 feet
2.note: tn Heb “thirty cubits.”

4.anchor: There were three rows of windows arranged in sets of three.
4.note: tn Heb “and framed [windows in] three rows, and opening to opening three times.” The precise meaning of this description is uncertain. Another option might be, “overhung [in] three rows.” This might mean they were positioned high on the walls.

5.anchor: All of the entrances
5.note: tn Heb “all of the doors and doorposts.”

5.anchor: were rectangular in shape
5.note: sn Rectangular in shape. That is, rather than arched.

5.anchor: and they were arranged in sets of three.
5.note: tn Heb “and all the entrances and the doorposts [had] four frames, and in front of opening to opening three times” (the precise meaning of the description is uncertain).

6.anchor: He made a colonnade
6.note: tn Heb “a porch of pillars.”

6.anchor: 75 feet
6.note: tn Heb “fifty cubits.”

6.anchor: long and 45 feet
6.note: tn Heb “thirty cubits.”

6.anchor: wide. There was a porch in front of this and pillars and a roof in front of the porch.
6.note: tn Heb “and a porch was in front of them (i.e., the aforementioned pillars) and pillars and a roof in front of them (i.e., the aforementioned pillars and porch).” The precise meaning of the term translated “roof” is uncertain; it occurs only here and in Ezek 41:25-26.

7.anchor: He also made a throne room, called “The Hall of Judgment,” where he made judicial decisions.
7.note: tn Heb “and a porch for the throne, where he was making judicial decisions, the Porch of Judgment, he made.”

7.anchor: It was paneled with cedar from the floor to the rafters.
7.note: tc The Hebrew text reads, “from the floor to the floor.” The second occurrence of the term הַקַּרְקָע (haqqarqa’, “the floor”) is probably an error; one should emend to הַקּוֹרוֹת (haqqorot, “the rafters”). See 6:16.

8.anchor: The palace where he lived was constructed in a similar way.
8.note: tn Heb “and his house where he lived, the other court [i.e., as opposed to the great court], separated from the house belonging to the hall, was like this work [i.e., this style of architecture].”

8.anchor: He also constructed a palace like this hall for Pharaoh’s daughter, whom he had married.
8.note: tn Heb “and a house he was making for the daughter of Pharaoh, whom Solomon had taken, like this porch.”

9.anchor: All of these were built with the best
9.note: tn Or “valuable” (see 5:17).

9.anchor: stones, chiseled to the right size
9.note: tn Heb “according to the measurement of chiseled [stone].”

9.anchor: and cut with a saw on all sides,
9.note: tn Heb “inside and out.”

9.anchor: from the foundation to the edge of the roof
9.note: tn The precise meaning of the Hebrew word טְפָחוֹת (tÿfakhot) is uncertain, but it is clear that the referent stands in opposition to the foundation.

10.anchor: The foundation was made of large valuable stones, measuring either 15 feet or 12 feet.
10.note: tn Heb “stones of ten cubits and stones of eight cubits” (it is unclear exactly what dimension is being measured). If both numbers refer to the length of the stones (cf. NCV, CEV, NLT), then perhaps stones of two different sizes were used in some alternating pattern.

11.anchor: Above the foundation
11.note: tn Heb “on top,” or “above.”

11.anchor: the best
11.note: tn Or “valuable” (see 5:17).

11.anchor: stones, chiseled to the right size,
11.note: tn Heb “according to the measurement of chiseled [stone].”

12.anchor: ’s temple and the hall of the palace.
12.note: tn Or “the porch of the temple.”

13.anchor: King Solomon sent for Hiram
13.note: tn Heb “King Solomon sent and took Hiram from Tyre.” In 2 Chr 2:13 (MT v. 12) and 4:11, 16 his name is spelled “Huram.”

13.anchor: of Tyre.
13.note: map For location see Map1-A2; Map2-G2; Map4-A1; JP3-F3; JP4-F3.

14.anchor: He was the son of a widow from the tribe of Naphtali,
14.note: tn 2 Chr 2:14 (13 HT) says “from the daughters of Dan.”

14.anchor: and his father was a craftsman in bronze from Tyre. He had the skill and knowledge
14.note: tn Heb “he was filled with the skill, understanding, and knowledge.”

15.anchor: He fashioned two bronze pillars; each pillar was 27 feet
15.note: tn Heb “eighteen cubits.”

15.anchor: high and 18 feet
15.note: tn Heb “twelve cubits.”

16.anchor: He made two bronze tops for the pillars; each was seven-and-a-half feet high.
16.note: tn Heb “two capitals he made to place on the tops of the pillars, cast in bronze; five cubits was the height of the first capital, and five cubits was the height of the second capital.”

17.anchor: The latticework on the tops of the pillars was adorned with ornamental wreaths and chains; the top of each pillar had seven groupings of ornaments.
17.note: tn Heb “there were seven for the first capital, and seven for the second capital.”

18.anchor: When he made the pillars, there were two rows of pomegranate-shaped ornaments around the latticework covering the top of each pillar.
18.note: tn Heb “he made the pillars, and two rows surrounding one latticework to cover the capitals which were on top of the pomegranates, and so he did for the second latticework.” The translation supplies “pomegranates” after “two rows,” and understands “pillars,” rather than “pomegranates,” to be the correct reading after “on top of.” The latter change finds support from many Hebrew mss and the ancient Greek version.

19.anchor: The tops of the two pillars in the porch were shaped like lilies and were six feet high.
19.note: tn Heb “the capitals which were on the top of the pillars were the work of lilies, in the porch, four cubits.” It is unclear exactly what dimension is being measured.

20.anchor: On the top of each pillar, right above the bulge beside the latticework, there were two hundred pomegranate-shaped ornaments arranged in rows all the way around.
20.note: tn Heb “and the capitals on the two pillars, also above, close beside the bulge which was beside the latticework, two hundred pomegranates in rows around, on the second capital.” The precise meaning of the word translated “bulge” is uncertain.

21.anchor: He set up the pillars on the porch in front of the main hall. He erected one pillar on the right
21.note: tn Or “south.”

21.anchor: side and called it Jakin;
21.note: sn The name Jakin appears to be a verbal form and probably means, “he establishes.”

21.anchor: he erected the other pillar on the left
21.note: tn Or “north.”

21.anchor: side and called it Boaz.
21.note: sn The meaning of the name Boaz is uncertain. For various proposals, see BDB 126-27 s.v. בעז. One attractive option is to revocalize the name as בְּעֹז (be’oz, “in strength”) and to understand it as completing the verbal form on the first pillar. Taking the words together and reading from right to left, one can translate the sentence, “he establishes [it] in strength.”

23.anchor: He also made the large bronze basin called “The Sea.”
23.note: tn Heb “He made the sea, cast.”sn This large basin that was mounted on twelve bronze bulls and contained water for the priests to bathe themselves (2 Chr 4:6; cf. Exod 30:17-21).

23.anchor: It measured 15 feet
23.note: tn Heb “ten cubits.”

23.anchor: from rim to rim, was circular in shape, and stood seven-and-a-half feet
23.note: tn Heb “five cubits.”

23.anchor: high. Its circumference was 45 feet.
23.note: tn Heb “and a measuring line went around it thirty cubits all around.”

24.anchor: Under the rim all the way around it
24.note: tn Heb “The Sea.” The proper noun has been replaced by the pronoun (“it”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.

24.anchor: were round ornaments
24.note: tn Or “gourd-shaped ornaments.”

24.anchor: arranged in settings 15 feet long.
24.note: tn Heb “ten cubits surrounding the sea all around.” The precise meaning of this description is uncertain.

24.anchor: The ornaments were in two rows and had been cast with “The Sea.”
24.note: tn Heb “the gourd-shaped ornaments were in two rows, cast in its casting.”

25.anchor: “The Sea” stood on top of twelve bulls. Three faced northward, three westward, three southward, and three eastward. “The Sea” was placed on top of them, and they all faced outward.
25.note: tn Heb “all their hindquarters were toward the inside.”

26.anchor: It was four fingers thick and its rim was like that of a cup shaped like a lily blossom. It could hold about 12,000 gallons.
26.note: tn Heb “two thousand baths” (a bath was a liquid measure roughly equivalent to six gallons).

27.anchor: He also made ten bronze movable stands. Each stand was six feet
27.note: tn Heb “four cubits.”

27.anchor: long, six feet
27.note: tn Heb “four cubits.”

27.anchor: wide, and four-and-a-half feet
27.note: tn Heb “three cubits.”

29.anchor: On these frames and joints were ornamental lions, bulls, and cherubs. Under the lions and bulls were decorative wreaths.
29.note: tn The precise meaning of these final words is uncertain. A possible literal translation would be, “wreaths, the work of descent.”

30.anchor: Each stand had four bronze wheels with bronze axles and four supports. Under the basin the supports were fashioned on each side with wreaths.
30.note: tn The precise meaning of this last word, translated “wreaths,” is uncertain.

31.anchor: Inside the stand was a round opening that was a foot-and-a-half deep; it had a support that was two and one-quarter feet long.
31.note: tn Heb “And its opening from the inside to the top and upwards [was] a cubit, and its opening was round, the work of a stand, a cubit-and-a-half.” The precise meaning of this description is uncertain.

31.anchor: On the edge of the opening were carvings in square frames.
31.note: tn Heb “also over its opening were carvings and their frames [were] squared, not round.”

32.anchor: The four wheels were under the frames and the crossbars of the axles were connected to the stand. Each wheel was two and one-quarter feet
32.note: tn Heb “a cubit-and-a-half” (a cubit was a unit of measure roughly equivalent to 18 inches or 45 cm).

34.anchor: Each stand had four supports, one per side projecting out from the stand.
34.note: tn Heb “four shoulders to the four sides of each stand, from the stand its shoulders.” The precise meaning of the description is uncertain.

35.anchor: On top of each stand was a round opening three-quarters of a foot deep;
35.note: tn Heb “and on top of the stand, a half cubit [in] height, round all around” (the meaning of this description is uncertain).

36.anchor: He engraved ornamental cherubs, lions, and palm trees on the plates of the supports and frames wherever there was room,
36.note: tn Heb “according to the space of each.”
tn The precise meaning of this last word, translated “wreaths,” is uncertain.

38.anchor: He also made ten bronze basins, each of which could hold about 240 gallons.
38.note: tn Heb “forty baths” (a bath was a liquid measure roughly equivalent to six gallons).

38.anchor: Each basin was six feet in diameter;
38.note: tn Heb “four cubits, each basin.” It is unclear which dimension is being measured.

40.anchor: Hiram also made basins, shovels, and bowls. He
40.note: tn Heb “Hiram.” The proper name has been replaced by the pronoun (“he”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.

40.anchor: ’s temple he had been assigned by King Solomon.
40.note: tn Heb “Hiram finished doing all the work which he did for King Solomon [on] the house of the Lord.”

41.note: tn The words “he made” are added for stylistic reasons.

44.anchor: the big bronze basin called “The Sea” with its twelve bulls underneath,

45.note: tn Heb “which Hiram made for King Solomon [for] the house of the Lord.”

46.anchor: The king had them cast in earth foundries

47.anchor: Solomon left all these items unweighed; there were so many of them they did not weigh the bronze.
47.note: tn Heb “Solomon left all the items, due to their very great abundance; the weight of the bronze was not sought.”

48.anchor: ’s temple: the gold altar, the gold table on which was kept the Bread of the Presence,
48.note: tn Heb “the bread of the face [or presence].” Many recent English versions employ “the bread of the Presence,” although this does not convey much to the modern reader.sn This bread was viewed as a perpetual offering to God and was regarded as holy. See Lev 24:5-9.

51.note: tn Heb “Solomon.” The proper name has been replaced by the pronoun (“he”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.

1.anchor: Solomon made an alliance by marriage with Pharaoh, king of Egypt; he married Pharaoh’s daughter. He brought her to the City of David
1.note: sn The phrase City of David refers here to the fortress of Zion in Jerusalem, not to Bethlehem. See 2 Sam 5:7.

1.anchor: and the wall around Jerusalem.
1.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

2.anchor: Now the people were offering sacrifices at the high places,
2.note: sn Offering sacrifices at the high places. The “high places” were places of worship that were naturally or artificially elevated.

tn Heb “for the name of the Lord.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor (thus the translation here, “to honor the Lord”). The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.

3.anchor: by following
3.note: tn Heb “Solomon loved the Lord by walking in.”

3.anchor: the practices
3.note: tn Or “policies, rules.”

4.anchor: The king went to Gibeon to offer sacrifices, for it had the most prominent of the high places.
4.note: tn Heb “for it was the great high place.”

4.anchor: Solomon would offer up
4.note: tn The verb form is an imperfect, which is probably used here in a customary sense to indicate continued or repeated action in past time. See GKC 314 §107.b.

5.anchor: appeared
5.note: tn Or “revealed himself.”

5.anchor: to Solomon in a dream. God said, “Tell
5.note: tn Heb “ask.”

6.anchor: Solomon replied, “You demonstrated
6.note: tn Heb “did.”

6.anchor: great loyalty to your servant, my father David, as he served
6.note: tn Heb “walked before.”

6.anchor: you faithfully, properly, and sincerely.
6.note: tn Heb “in faithfulness and in innocence and in uprightness of heart with you.”

6.anchor: You have maintained this great loyalty to this day by allowing his son to sit on his throne.
6.note: tn Heb “and you have kept to him this great loyalty and you gave to him a son [who] sits on his throne as this day.”

7.anchor: my God, you have made your servant king in my father David’s place, even though I am only a young man and am inexperienced.
7.note: tn Heb “and I do not know going out or coming in.”

8.anchor: Your servant stands
8.note: tn There is no verb expressed in the Hebrew text; “stands” is supplied in the translation for clarification.

8.anchor: among your chosen people;
8.note: tn Heb “your people whom you have chosen.”

9.anchor: So give your servant a discerning mind
9.note: tn Heb “a hearing heart.” (The Hebrew term translated “heart” often refers to the mental faculties.)

9.anchor: so he can make judicial decisions for
9.note: tn Heb “to judge.”

9.anchor: your people and distinguish right from wrong.
9.note: tn Heb “to understand between good and evil.”

9.anchor: Otherwise
9.note: tn Heb “for”; the word “otherwise” is used to reflect the logical sense of the statement.

9.anchor: no one is able
9.note: tn Heb “who is able?” The rhetorical question anticipates the answer, “no one.”

9.anchor: to make judicial decisions for
9.note: tn Heb “to judge.”

9.anchor: this great nation of yours.”
9.note: tn Heb “your numerous people.”

10.anchor: The Lord
10.note: tn The Hebrew term translated “Lord” here and in v.15 is אֲדֹנָי (’adonay).

10.anchor: was pleased that Solomon made this request.
10.note: tn Heb “And the thing was good in the eyes of the Lord, for Solomon asked for this thing.”

11.anchor: God said to him, “Because you asked for the ability to make wise judicial decisions, and not for long life, or riches, or vengeance on your enemies,
11.note: tn Heb “because you asked for this thing, and did not ask for yourself many days and did not ask for yourself riches and did not ask for the life of your enemies, but you asked for yourself understanding to hear judgment.”

12.anchor: I
12.note: tn This statement is introduced in the Hebrew text by the particle הִנֵּה (hinneh, “look”) which draws attention to and emphasizes what follows.

12.anchor: grant your request,
12.note: tn Heb “I am doing according to your words.” The perfect tense is sometimes used of actions occurring at the same time a statement is made.

12.anchor: and give
12.note: tn This statement is introduced by the particle הִנֵּה (hinneh, “look”) which draws attention to and emphasizes what follows. The translation assumes that the perfect tense here indicates that the action occurs as the statement is made (i.e., “right now I give you”).

12.anchor: you a wise and discerning mind
12.note: tn Heb “heart.” (The Hebrew term translated “heart” often refers to the mental faculties.)

12.anchor: superior to that of anyone who has preceded or will succeed you.
12.note: tn Heb “so that there has not been one like you prior to you, and after you one will not arise like you.”

13.anchor: Furthermore, I am giving
13.note: tn The translation assumes that the perfect tense here indicates that the action occurs as the statement is made.

13.anchor: you what you did not request – riches and honor so that you will be the greatest king of your generation.
13.note: tn Heb “so that there is not one among the kings like you all your days.” The LXX lacks the words “all your days.”

14.anchor: If you follow my instructions
14.note: tn Heb “walk in my ways.”

14.anchor: by obeying
14.note: tn Or “keeping.”

14.anchor: my rules and regulations, just as your father David did,
14.note: tn Heb “walked.”

14.anchor: then I will grant you long life.”
14.note: tn Heb “I will lengthen your days.”

15.anchor: Solomon then woke up and realized it was a dream.
15.note: tn Heb “and look, a dream.”

15.anchor: He went to Jerusalem, stood before the ark of the Lord’s covenant, offered up burnt sacrifices, presented peace offerings,
15.note: tn Or “tokens of peace”; NIV, TEV “fellowship offerings.”

18.anchor: Then three days after I had my baby, this woman also had a baby. We were alone; there was no one else in the house except the two of us.
18.note: sn There was no one else in the house except the two of us. In other words, there were no other witnesses to the births who could identify which child belonged to which mother.

19.anchor: This woman’s child suffocated
19.note: tn Heb “died.”

19.anchor: during the night when she rolled
19.note: tn Heb “lay, slept.”

21.anchor: I got up in the morning to nurse my son, and there he was,
21.note: tn Heb “look.”

21.anchor: dead! But when I examined him carefully in the morning, I realized it was not my baby.”
21.note: tn Heb “look, it was not my son to whom I had given birth.”

22.anchor: The other woman said, “No! My son is alive; your son is dead!” But the first woman replied, “No, your son is dead; my son is alive.” Each presented her case before the king.
22.note: tn Heb “they spoke before the king.” Another option is to translate, “they argued before the king.”

26.anchor: The real mother
26.note: tn Heb “the woman whose son was alive.”

26.anchor: spoke up to the king, for her motherly instincts were aroused.
26.note: tn Heb “for her compassions grew warm for her son.”

26.anchor: She said, “My master, give her the living child! Whatever you do, don’t kill him!”
26.note: tn The infinitive absolute before the negated jussive emphasizes the main verb.

28.anchor: When all Israel heard about the judicial decision which the king had rendered, they respected
28.note: tn Heb “feared,” perhaps in the sense, “stood in awe of.”

28.anchor: the king, for they realized
28.note: tn Heb “saw.”

28.anchor: that he possessed supernatural wisdom
28.note: tn Heb “the wisdom of God within him.”

3.anchor: Elihoreph and Ahijah, the sons of Shisha, wrote down what happened.
3.note: tn Heb “were scribes”; NASB, NIV, NRSV “secretaries”; TEV, NLT “court secretaries.”

4.anchor: Benaiah son of Jehoiada was commander of
4.note: tn Heb “was over.”

5.anchor: Zabud son of Nathan was a priest and adviser to
5.note: tn Heb “close associate of”; KJV, ASV, NASB “the king’s friend” (a title for an adviser, not just an acquaintance).

6.anchor: Adoniram son of Abda was supervisor of
6.note: tn Heb “was over.”

6.anchor: the work crews.
6.note: sn The work crews. This Hebrew word (מַס, mas) refers to a group of laborers conscripted for royal or public service.

12.anchor: Baana son of Ahilud was in charge of Taanach and Megiddo,
12.note: map For location see Map1-D4; Map2-C1; Map4-C2; Map5-F2; Map7-B1.

21.note: sn Beginning with 4:21, the verse numbers through 5:18 in the English Bible differ from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 4:21 ET = 5:1 HT, 4:22 ET = 5:2 HT, etc., through 5:18 ET = 5:32 HT. Beginning with 6:1 the numbering of verses in the English Bible and the Hebrew text is again the same.

21.anchor: Solomon ruled all the kingdoms from the Euphrates River
21.note: tn Heb “the River” (also in v. 24). This is the standard designation for the Euphrates River in biblical Hebrew.

21.anchor: to the land of the Philistines, as far as the border of Egypt. These kingdoms paid tribute as Solomon’s subjects throughout his lifetime.
21.note: tn Heb “[They] were bringing tribute and were serving Solomon all the days of his life.”

22.anchor: Each day Solomon’s royal court consumed
22.note: tn Heb “the food of Solomon for each day was.”

22.anchor: thirty cors
22.note: tn As a unit of dry measure a cor was roughly equivalent to six bushels.

23.anchor: ten calves fattened in the stall,
23.note: tn The words “in the stall” are added for clarification; note the immediately following reference to cattle from the pasture.

24.anchor: His royal court was so large because
24.note: tn Heb “because.” The words “his royal court was so large” are added to facilitate the logical connection with the preceding verse.

24.anchor: he ruled over all the kingdoms west of the Euphrates River from Tiphsah
24.note: sn Tiphsah. This was located on the Euphrates River.

24.anchor: to Gaza; he was at peace with all his neighbors.
24.note: tn Heb “for he was ruling over all [the region] beyond the River, from Tiphsah to Gaza, over all the kingdoms beyond the River, and he had peace on every side all around.”

25.anchor: All the people of Judah and Israel had security; everyone from Dan to Beer Sheba enjoyed the produce of their vines and fig trees throughout Solomon’s lifetime.
25.note: tn Heb “Judah and Israel lived securely, each one under his vine and under his fig tree, from Dan to Beer Sheba, all the days of Solomon.”

26.anchor: Solomon had 4,000
26.note: tn The Hebrew text has “40,000,” but this is probably an inflated number (nevertheless it is followed by KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV, TEV, CEV). Some Greek mss of the OT and the parallel in 2 Chr 9:25 read “4,000” (cf. NAB, NIV, NCV, NLT).

27.anchor: The district governors acquired supplies for King Solomon and all who ate in his royal palace.
27.note: tn Heb “everyone who drew near to the table of King Solomon.”

28.anchor: Each one also brought to the assigned location his quota of barley and straw for the various horses.
28.note: tn Heb “barley and straw for the horses and the steeds they brought to the place which was there, each according to his measure.”

29.anchor: God gave Solomon wisdom and very great discernment; the breadth of his understanding
29.note: tn Heb “heart,” i.e., mind. (The Hebrew term translated “heart” often refers to the mental faculties.)

30.anchor: Solomon was wiser than all the men of the east and all the sages of Egypt.
30.note: tn Heb “the wisdom of Solomon was greater than the wisdom of all the sons of the east and all the wisdom of Egypt.”

31.anchor: He was wiser than any man, including Ethan the Ezrahite or Heman, Calcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol. He was famous in all the neighboring nations.
31.note: tn Heb “his name was in all the surrounding nations.”

32.note: tn Heb “spoke.”

33.anchor: He produced manuals on botany, describing every kind of plant,
33.note: tn Heb “he spoke about plants.”

33.anchor: from the cedars of Lebanon to the hyssop that grows on walls. He also produced manuals on biology, describing
33.note: tn Heb “he spoke about.”

34.anchor: People from all nations came to hear Solomon’s display of wisdom;
34.note: tn Heb “the wisdom of Solomon.”

1.note: sn The verse numbers in the English Bible differ from those in the Hebrew text (BHS) here; 5:1-18 in the English Bible corresponds to 5:15-32 in the Hebrew text. See the note at 4:21.

1.anchor: King Hiram of Tyre

1.anchor: sent messengers
1.note: tn Heb “his servants.”

3.anchor: Lord
3.note: tn Heb “a house for the name of the Lord.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor. The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.

3.anchor: subdued his enemies.
3.note: tn Heb “because of the battles which surrounded him until the Lord placed them under the soles of his feet.”

5.anchor: So I have decided
5.note: tn Heb “Look, I am saying.”

5.anchor: Lord
5.note: tn Heb “a house for the name of the Lord.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor. The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.

5.anchor: instructed my father David, ‘Your son, whom I will put on your throne in your place, is the one who will build a temple to honor me.’
5.note: tn Heb “a house for my name.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor. The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.

7.anchor: is worthy of praise today because he
7.note: tn Or “Blessed be the Lord today, who….”

8.anchor: Hiram then sent this message to Solomon: “I received
8.note: tn Heb “heard.”

8.anchor: the message you sent to me. I will give you all the cedars and evergreens you need.
8.note: tn Heb “I will satisfy all your desire with respect to cedar wood and with respect to the wood of evergreens.”

9.anchor: My servants will bring the timber down from Lebanon to the sea. I will send it by sea in raft-like bundles to the place you designate.
9.note: tn Heb “I will place them [on? as?] rafts in the sea to the place where you designate to me.” This may mean he would send them by raft, or that he would tie them in raft-like bundles, and have ships tow them down to an Israelite port.

30.note: tn Heb “and you, hear inside your dwelling place, inside heaven.” The precise nuance of the preposition אֶל (’el), used here with the verb “hear,” is unclear. One expects the preposition “from,” which appears in the parallel text in 2 Chr 6:21. The nuance “inside; among” is attested for אֶל (see Gen 23:19; 1 Sam 10:22; Jer 4:3), but in each case a verb of motion is employed with the preposition, unlike 1 Kgs 8:30. The translation above (“from inside”) is based on the demands of the immediate context rather than attested usage elsewhere.

31.anchor: “When someone is accused of sinning against his neighbor and the latter pronounces a curse on the alleged offender before your altar in this temple, be willing to forgive the accused if the accusation is false.
31.note: tn Heb “and forgive the man who sins against his neighbor when one takes up against him a curse to curse him and the curse comes before your altar in this house.” In the Hebrew text the words “and forgive” conclude v. 30, but the accusative sign at the beginning of v. 31 suggests the verb actually goes with what follows in v. 31. The parallel text in 2 Chr 6:22 begins with “and if,” rather than the accusative sign. In this case “forgive” must be taken with what precedes, and v. 31 must be taken as the protasis (“if” clause) of a conditional sentence, with v. 32 being the apodosis (“then” clause) that completes the sentence.sn Be willing to forgive the accused if the accusation is false. At first it appears that Solomon is asking God to forgive the guilty party. But in v. 32 Solomon asks the Lord to discern who is guilty and innocent, so v. 31 must refer to a situation where an accusation has been made, but not yet proven. The very periphrastic translation reflects this interpretation.

51.note: tn The Hebrew term כּוּר (kur, “furnace,” cf. Akkadian ku„ru) is a metaphor for the intense heat of purification. A כּוּר was not a source of heat but a crucible (“iron-smelting furnace”) in which precious metals were melted down and their impurities burned away (see I. Cornelius, NIDOTTE 2:618-19). Thus Egypt served not as a place of punishment for the Israelites, but as a place of refinement to bring Israel to a place of submission to divine sovereignty.sn From the middle of the iron-smelting furnace. The metaphor of a furnace suggests fire and heat and is an apt image to remind the people of the suffering they endured while slaves in Egypt.

For various options see HALOT 871 s.v. עצר 6 and M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 107. The two terms are usually taken as polar opposites (“slaves and freemen” or “minors and adults”), but Cogan and Tadmor, on the basis of contextual considerations (note the usage with אֶפֶס [’efes], “nothing but”) in Deut 32:36 and 2 Kgs 14:26, argue convincingly that the terms are synonyms, meaning “restrained and abandoned,” and refer to incapable or incapacitated individuals.

10.note: tn The traditional view understands the verb בָּעַר (ba’ar) to mean “burn.” Manure was sometimes used as fuel (see Ezek 4:12, 15). However, an alternate view takes בָּעַר as a homonym meaning “sweep away” (HALOT 146 s.v. II בער). In this case one might translate, “I will sweep away the dynasty of Jeroboam, just as one sweeps away manure it is gone” (cf. ASV, NASB, TEV). Either metaphor emphasizes the thorough and destructive nature of the coming judgment.

24.anchor: really does speak through you.”
24.note: tn Heb “you are a man of God and the word of the Lord is truly in your mouth.”sn This episode is especially significant in light of Ahab’s decision to promote Baal worship in Israel. In Canaanite mythology the drought that swept over the region (v. 1) would signal that Baal, a fertility god responsible for providing food for his subjects, had been defeated by the god of death and was imprisoned in the underworld. While Baal was overcome by death and unable to function like a king, Israel’s God demonstrated his sovereignty and superiority to death by providing food for a widow and restoring life to her son. And he did it all in Sidonian territory, Baal’s back yard, as it were. The episode demonstrates that Israel’s God, not Baal, is the true king who provides food and controls life and death. This polemic against Baalism reaches its climax in the next chapter, when the Lord proves that he, not Baal, controls the elements of the storm and determines when the rains will fall.

3.anchor: and 300 concubines;
3.note: sn Concubines were slave women in ancient Near Eastern societies who were the legal property of their master, but who could have legitimate sexual relations with their master. A concubine’s status was more elevated than a mere servant, but she was not free and did not have the legal rights of a free wife. The children of a concubine could, in some instances, become equal heirs with the children of the free wife. The usage in the present passage suggests that after the period of the Judges concubines may have become more of a royal prerogative (cf. also 2 Sam 21:10-14).

15.anchor: will hand it over to the king.”
15.note: sn “Attack! You will succeed; the Lord will hand it over to the king.” One does not expect Micaiah, having just vowed to speak only what the Lord tells him, to agree with the other prophets and give the king an inaccurate prophecy. Micaiah’s actions became understandable later, when it is revealed that the Lord desires to deceive the king and lead him to his demise. The Lord even dispatches a lying spirit to deceive Ahab’s prophets. Micaiah can lie to the king because he realizes this lie is from the Lord. It is important to note that in v. 14 Micaiah only vows to speak the word of the Lord; he does not necessarily say he will tell the truth. In this case the Lord’s word itself is deceptive. Only when the king adjures him to tell the truth (v. 16), does Micaiah do so.

21.anchor: Then a spirit
21.note: tn Heb “the spirit.” The significance of the article prefixed to רוּחַ (ruakh) is uncertain, but it could contain a clue as to this spirit’s identity, especially when interpreted in light of v. 24. It is certainly possible, and probably even likely, that the article is used in a generic or dramatic sense and should be translated, “a spirit.” In the latter case it would show that this spirit was vivid and definite in the mind of Micaiah the storyteller. However, if one insists that the article indicates a well-known or universally known spirit, the following context provides a likely referent. Verse 24 tells how Zedekiah slapped Micaiah in the face and then asked sarcastically, “Which way did the spirit from the Lord (רוּחַ־יְהוָה, [ruakh-Yahweh], Heb “the spirit of the Lord”) go when he went from me to speak to you?” When the phrase “the spirit of the Lord” refers to the divine spirit (rather than the divine breath or mind, Isa 40:7, 13) elsewhere, the spirit energizes an individual or group for special tasks or moves one to prophesy. This raises the possibility that the deceiving spirit of vv. 20-23 is the same as the divine spirit mentioned by Zedekiah in v. 24. This would explain why the article is used on רוּחַ; he can be called “the spirit” because he is the well-known spirit who energizes the prophets.

22.anchor: said, ‘Deceive and overpower him.
22.note: tn The Hebrew text has two imperfects connected by וְגַם (vÿgam). These verbs could be translated as specific futures, “you will deceive and also you will prevail,” in which case the Lord is assuring the spirit of success on his mission. However, in a commissioning context (note the following imperatives) such as this, it is more likely that the imperfects are injunctive, in which case one could translate, “Deceive, and also overpower.”
